message:
fix(storage): repair integer-typed datetimes + legacy 'done' status on load, plus v6 migration

Two cooperating bug fixes + a schema migration that closes the
root-cause drift.

Bug 1 — datetime storage-class silently lossy:
External tools, older migration paths, and direct SQLite edits have
occasionally written integer epoch microseconds (16-digit i64 from
chrono `.timestamp_micros()`) into `issues.{created_at, updated_at,
closed_at, due_at, defer_until, deleted_at, compacted_at}`. The
reader used `row.get(idx).and_then(SqliteValue::as_text).unwrap_or("")`
and then routed the empty-string result through `parse_datetime`,
which returned `UNIX_EPOCH`. Effect: every affected row's datetime
became 1970-01-01 on read, then exported as 1970-01-01 to JSONL —
silent corruption on export.

Bug 2 — legacy 'done' / 'completed' statuses accepted but wrong:
JSONL imported from Go-beads (bd) and some third-party tools carries
statuses like `"done"`, `"complete"`, `"completed"`, `"finished"`,
`"resolved"`. Our `Status` deserializer falls back to `Custom(raw)`
for unknown variants, which survives the in-memory round trip but:
(a) our own `is_terminal()` returns false for Custom, so the
`closed_at` repair path skips these rows; (b) the DB CHECK
constraint (`status='closed' AND closed_at IS NOT NULL`) then
rejects them on write; (c) downstream tools (bv, bd-style readers)
reject the unknown status outright.

Fix 1 (reader — src/storage/sqlite.rs):
- Introduce `parse_datetime_value(Option<&SqliteValue>)` and
  `parse_opt_datetime_value(...)` helpers that accept the full
  SQLite storage-class spectrum:
    * Text → existing RFC 3339 parser.
    * Integer → `datetime_from_epoch_auto(i64)` which auto-detects
      unit from magnitude: `<10^10` is seconds, `<10^13` is ms,
      `<10^16` is µs, otherwise ns. Non-overlapping ranges because
      any realistic beads timestamp is within a century of the
      Unix epoch.
    * Float → `datetime_from_epoch_seconds_f64` treats the value as
      fractional seconds; rejects non-finite and out-of-i64 range.
    * Blob → explicit Config error.
    * Null / missing → `UNIX_EPOCH` (for required datetimes) or
      `None` (for optional ones). Empty text also → `None` in the
      optional path so round-trips stay idempotent.
- Thread the new helpers through every datetime read path:
    * `get_issue_from_conn` (primary path).
    * `get_closed_issue_from_conn`.
    * The stats query row reader.
    * The dependency loader.
    * The issue-metadata bulk loader.
  The old `get_str_ref` closure is removed from each function that
  used it exclusively for datetime columns.
- Helper `epoch_remainder_u32` centralizes the negative-remainder
  / range-check for non-second subunits so each branch above stays
  one line.

Fix 2 (schema migration — src/storage/schema.rs):
- Bump `CURRENT_SCHEMA_VERSION` from 5 to 6.
- Add a v6 migration branch that, for databases with an `issues`
  table, runs:
    * `repair_integer_datetime_columns(conn)` — a per-column UPDATE
      that rewrites any INTEGER datetime into canonical RFC 3339
      text via `strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%f', col/1000000.0,
      'unixepoch') || 'Z'`. Gated on `typeof(col) = 'integer'` so
      it's idempotent and never touches already-text rows. Runs
      across all seven datetime columns.
    * `repair_legacy_status_values(conn)` — a single UPDATE that
      maps `LOWER(status) IN ('done','complete','completed',
      'finished','resolved')` to `'closed'` and ensures `closed_at
      = COALESCE(closed_at, updated_at, created_at)` so the CHECK
      constraint is satisfied.
- The in-code comment on the v6 branch spells out the two root
  causes and why the reader-side coercion plus migration are both
  needed (migrations fix the at-rest state; reader coercion keeps
  future-stray writers from re-corrupting it).

Tracing log line at `info` level announces the v6 migration so the
operator sees when it kicks in.

No user-facing API change. Existing tests still pass because the
canonical-text path is unchanged; corruption recovery is new
behaviour.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
